designs.title: Persephone Cafe
designs.tagline: A little sugar, a little spice
designs.edition: Open edition · Bless Your Heart imprint
designs.placard: Seasonal establishment. Open six months of the year, closed the other six, per the terms of an arrangement the management declines to discuss. The pie is pomegranate. It is always pomegranate.
designs.audioGuide: The lowland pomegranate pie, served seasonally — six months on, six months off. The proprietor maintains she was taken against her will. The regulars note she returns every autumn, precisely on schedule, and has never once missed a shift. Draw your own conclusions. Her mother certainly has.

designs.title: Artemis — No Peeking
designs.placard: The goddess of the hunt at full draw. The stag at right is a recent acquisition — formerly a hunter named Actaeon, reassigned after an incident at the bathing pool.
designs.audioGuide: Here we observe the goddess of the hunt at full draw. The stag, rear of frame, was until quite recently a young man who saw something he should not have. His own hounds will no longer recognize him. The goddess does not miss, and she does not forgive — for her, these are the same skill.

designs.title: Nizari & The Ismailis
designs.placard: Reads as a touring act. Functions as a warning. The dagger and note were left on the sultan's pillow while he slept among his guards. The siege was lifted shortly after.
designs.audioGuide: We are looking at a negotiation. While Saladin slept, ringed by his guards, the Assassins left a dagger and a note upon his pillow. No blood was drawn — none was required. He withdrew his campaign against them shortly thereafter. Some messages are simply received.

designs.title: Djinn and Toxic
designs.placard: Promotional broadside for a touring act of disputed existence. Venue list is authentic. Wishes granted at the merch table, worded carefully or not at all.
designs.audioGuide: An apex wish-granter, observed mid-emergence. It has had several thousand years to perfect the fine print, and it shows. The tour is called Sinners' Choice for a reason: every wish is granted exactly as worded. Exactly as worded.

designs.title: Show Me Your Rudis
designs.placard: A wooden sword, useless in combat and priceless everywhere else. Presented once, on the day a gladiator wins his freedom. The only sword that ever ended a fight permanently.
designs.audioGuide: What you see is a wooden sword — utterly worthless as a weapon. And yet no gladiator ever held anything more valuable. It is presented exactly once, on the day a man earns his freedom. Which makes it, quite literally, the only sword that ever ended a fight for good.
designs.conservatorNote: Conservator's note: headline cropped in printing; letterspacing collapsed in the maker's mark. Full re-typeset scheduled before release.

designs.title: Decimation
designs.placard: Roman unit-cohesion policy, reviewed favorably by survivors. When a legion broke, one man in ten was chosen by lot and dispatched by the other nine. Morale, curiously, improved.
designs.audioGuide: Roman discipline, captured at its most arithmetic. When a legion broke and ran, every tenth man was chosen by lot and dealt with by the remaining nine. The survivors' reviews were, understandably, mixed. Nine out of ten. As it were.
designs.conservatorNote: Conservator's note: headline clipped at the top margin; comma spacing collapsed in the lower lockup. Re-typeset before sale.

designs.title: Grace O'Malley
designs.edition: Bless Your Heart · Badass Women of History
designs.placard: Pirate queen of Connacht, shown at collection time. Sailed to meet Elizabeth I as an equal, refused to bow, negotiated as a head of state. The ledgers suggest she was owed.
designs.audioGuide: The pirate queen of Connacht, observed mid-collection. She once sailed up the Thames to meet Elizabeth the First face to face, refused to bow, and bargained as one sovereign to another. The English called her a brigand. Her ledgers suggest the debt ran the other direction.
designs.conservatorNote: Conservator's note: spelling fault in the lower text — HAWE for HAVE. One letterform stands between this piece and the floor.

designs.title: Medusa
designs.region: The temple of Athena, then exile
designs.placard: Approach from behind, or not at all. She did not choose the snakes — they were the punishment for being wronged in the wrong temple. She keeps her eyes closed here. A courtesy.
designs.audioGuide: Approach from behind, or not at all. She did not ask for the snakes; they were her punishment for the crime of being wronged in the wrong temple. Perseus brought a mirror. Notice that here, her eyes are closed — a courtesy. She extends more mercy than she was ever shown.
designs.conservatorNote: Conservator's note: tagline printed twice — reads as a press error, not emphasis. The mirror element is ambiguous; visitors report seeing a bicycle wheel. Composition revision recommended.

designs.title: Seafoam Beauty
designs.placard: The goddess of love, surfacing in her preferred habitat: someone else's line of sight. The war god at rear believes the encounter is going well for him.
designs.audioGuide: Born of seafoam, the goddess of love surfaces here in her natural habitat — someone else's field of vision. Note the god of war, rear of frame, who believes this encounter is going rather well for him. Now observe her expression. It is not.

designs.title: Boudica
designs.region: Iceni lands, relocated to the Lowcountry
designs.placard: The Iceni queen among the live oaks and Spanish moss, where the climate suits a long grudge. Rome sent auditors. She sent Rome a bill, payable in three cities.
designs.audioGuide: The Iceni queen, transplanted here to the live oaks and the Spanish moss, where the climate better suits a long grudge. Rome flogged her, wronged her daughters, and seized her lands — an audit, they called it. She burned three of their cities in reply. The invoice was considered settled.
designs.conservatorNote: Curator's memo: strongest illustration in the collection; the line beneath it is a category, not a caption. Awaiting one sentence of word golf worthy of her.

designs.title: Organism 46B
designs.region: Lake Vostok, beneath two miles of ice
designs.placard: Recovered from beneath the Antarctic ice, allegedly. The containment glass is cracked, the sign says keep out, and the specimen only wants affection. These facts are related.
designs.audioGuide: Recovered, allegedly, from a lake beneath two miles of Antarctic ice. The specimen displays remarkable intelligence, a great many arms, and a single documented desire. The glass is cracked. The sign says keep out. It only wants to be loved. These facts are related.
designs.conservatorNote: Curator's memo: rendered in deep-water blues, outside the house palette. Either it anchors a new wing or it is drift. The committee has not decided.

designs.title: Anne & Mary
designs.medium: Silhouette print on cream, cutlass and flintlock in profile
designs.edition: Two granted, gallows left holding the rope
designs.placard: Two pirates stood before a court that had already measured the rope, and each produced the eighteenth century's only reliable legal defense. The noose between them is depicted unemployed. The court's records do not preserve the judge's expression, which historians consider a loss.
designs.audioGuide: Observe the two silhouettes, back to back, armed, and — crucially for our purposes — expecting. English law would not hang a pregnant woman, a policy Anne Bonny and Mary Read invoked with impeccable timing. It remains the only recorded instance of maternity leave being granted by a hanging judge. The gallows, visible at center, was asked to wait indefinitely.
designs.conservatorNote: Upper text block is cropped at the top margin of the print; letterforms partially lost at the trim line. Under evaluation for reprint.

designs.title: Anne Bonny
designs.placard: When Calico Jack was captured because his crew was too drunk to fight, Anne Bonny — who had fought — offered her assessment at his hanging: had he fought like a man, he need not have died like a dog. This print translates the sentiment into Southern. The noose, cutlass, pistol, and crow are all witnesses for the prosecution.
designs.audioGuide: Anne Bonny escaped the gallows by pleading her belly, then escaped the historical record entirely — no execution, no grave, no ending. The Spanish moss dripping from the title is a Crossroad signature: the Golden Age of Piracy filtered through a Lowcountry porch. 'Bless your heart' is, as ever, not a blessing. Jack understood it perfectly. Briefly.
designs.conservatorNote: The impression carries a compositor's error in the closing line — 'BLESS YOUR, HEART, JACK' — with the first comma displaced. The registrar has elected to preserve the error; Anne would have wanted Jack to see it.

designs.title: Aztec
designs.region: A bayou that has clearly seen things, temple adjacent
designs.medium: Woodcut on black in cream and ember orange, heart at center
designs.edition: One per vegetable, extracted with ceremony
designs.placard: An artichoke is opened to reveal an anatomically committed heart, observed by a plantation house, a chapel, an alligator, and a skull, none of which intervene. The composition relocates Mesoamerican ritual to a Southern swamp with no explanation offered. The sentiment is technically comforting.
designs.audioGuide: The Aztec priesthood and the produce section agree on one point: the heart is the prize, and reaching it takes dedication. Here the extraction is botanical, which the surrounding swamp seems to regard as a welcome change of pace. Note the rowboat crossing the ember-lit water, its occupant unbothered. The phrase itself is offered in the spirit of this wing — a kindness with a blade in it.
designs.conservatorNote: Banner text order garbled in printing — reads 'AZTEC EVEN / ARTICHOKES' across the top scroll where 'AZTEC / EVEN ARTICHOKES' was intended. Held for study.

designs.title: AZTEC
designs.placard: An artichoke enshrined at the center of the calendar stone, an honor traditionally reserved for the sun. The substitution was not explained. The sentiment — that even the most armored things have hearts — was considered self-evident and is not discussed further on this placard.
designs.audioGuide: At the heart of the great calendar, where the fifth sun should burn, sits a vegetable known primarily for being difficult to get into. The parallel is intentional. Companion piece to the nocturne hanging nearby; the registrar has hung them in the same wing so they can see each other.

designs.title: Bless Your Heart
designs.tagline: The Queen, the goat, and the tree that saw everything
designs.era: Undated; the crown suggests earlier, the attitude suggests timeless
designs.region: A throne room somewhere below the Mason-Dixon line
designs.medium: Charcoal and graphite on paper, applied with visible disapproval
designs.placard: A crowned figure regards the viewer with the particular warmth reserved for someone who has just said something foolish at a church supper. The goat, scholars agree, is not amused either. The banner clarifies that this is a blessing, technically.
designs.audioGuide: Observe the posture of the sovereign: erect, patient, entirely without mercy. In the wild, this phrase is deployed moments before a reputation is quietly dismantled. The goat serves no ceremonial function that we can identify, and yet no one has dared ask it to leave. Notice how the bare tree offers no shelter — a detail the artist surely intended.

designs.title: Ching Shih — More Boats, More Problems
designs.region: The South China Sea, all of it
designs.placard: Ching Shih commanded tens of thousands of pirates and a fleet the Qing navy, the East India Company, and the Portuguese Empire each failed to defeat. She then negotiated her own amnesty, kept the money, and retired peacefully — the only pirate career in history with a satisfying final chapter. The problems, it turns out, were everyone else's.
designs.audioGuide: Count the sails. Now imagine being the imperial official assigned to stop them. Ching Shih's code of conduct was stricter than the navy's and enforced considerably more reliably, which is one of several reasons she won.

designs.title: EVEN ARTICHOKES HAVE HEARTS
designs.tagline: A nocturne in cream on black
designs.region: A farmhouse near the calendar stone
designs.placard: A dense devotional scene in which two artichokes are revealed, anatomically, to contain hearts — a fact the vegetable community has long insisted upon. The Aztec calendar stone presides. The skulls are decorative, mostly.
designs.audioGuide: Everything in this composition is either alive, was alive, or is a vegetable, and the artist declines to say which is which. Note the anatomical hearts nested in the artichokes, rendered with the tenderness usually reserved for saints. The farmhouse in the background has seen things. The moon, as ever, says nothing.

designs.title: Olga of Kiev
designs.placard: Widowed by the Drevlians, Olga responded with a series of increasingly creative funerals — theirs. She later converted to Christianity and was made a saint, proving the Church keeps excellent receipts but forgives with style. The burning churches in the background are theirs, not hers; hers came later, and stayed standing.
designs.audioGuide: The veil reads as piety. The onion domes behind her, engulfed, read as context. Olga of Kiev is venerated as Equal-to-the-Apostles, which is the highest honor available to a woman who once buried a diplomatic delegation alive and considered the matter settled. The composition asks you to hold both facts at once. She certainly did.

designs.title: CALIGULA
designs.medium: Woodcut after a cracked marble original
designs.placard: A portrait bust of the emperor as his contemporaries described him in public: fine, totally fine. The crackle pattern in the marble is original. So was the horse in the Senate. Sources from within stabbing distance report no concerns whatsoever.
designs.audioGuide: The laurel sprigs flanking the bust were added by an assistant who wished to keep his position, and his head. Note the serene expression, achieved by every sculptor who valued continuity of employment. The reign lasted four years. The reviews were published posthumously — the reviewers', not his. Well. Eventually his, too.

designs.title: Cerberus
designs.region: The gates of the Underworld, reception desk
designs.medium: Fine-line engraving on tan, chains rendered broken
designs.edition: One of one; three of three heads
designs.placard: The hound of Hades is depicted with its chains snapped and one head winking, a security posture the Underworld's management has declined to address. Of the three heads, one is alert, one is delighted, and one is skeptical, which collectively covers most situations. The title is literal.
designs.audioGuide: Cerberus holds the distinction of being the only guard dog whose job was keeping souls in rather than intruders out. Heracles famously wrestled him to the surface as the last and most humiliating item on a twelve-point checklist. Observe the broken chains at each side — evidence of either great strength or lax facilities management. Every dog under the world, it turns out, is an underdog.

designs.title: Check Your Box, Pandora
designs.tagline: Hope was in there the whole time
designs.era: The beginning of everything going wrong
designs.placard: The container — a jar, in the original, though the mistranslation stuck — released every affliction known to humanity. One thing remained inside. The Archive presents this piece as a reminder to read the entire contents before opening, a lesson also applicable to email.
designs.audioGuide: Notice the single word visible within: HOPE. Scholars have debated for millennia whether its containment was mercy or the final cruelty. The gift shop takes no position.

designs.title: DJINN & TOXIC — ONE WISH TOO MANY
DJINN AND TOXIC
designs.placard: A second state of the Archive's DJINN & TOXIC, here as a parchment poster. The genie stands arms crossed above the classic lamp, wearing the expression of a customer-service professional on wish number three. Whether this preceded or followed the principal work is a matter of ongoing study.
designs.audioGuide: Compare this sheet with the principal work hanging nearby. The bottle has become a lamp, the crescent moon has been deaccessioned, and the genie's patience has visibly declined between states. Print historians call this progression; the genie, we suspect, calls it Tuesday. Folklore is unanimous that the third wish is where things go wrong. His arms suggest someone is on their fourth.

designs.title: DODECAHEDRRONS
designs.region: Scattered across the northern provinces, deliberately
designs.placard: A legionary distributes small bronze objects of no recorded purpose across three provinces, for reasons the artifact's title makes clear. Over one hundred have been excavated. None have been explained. The score stands.
designs.audioGuide: The Roman dodecahedron appears in no text, no ledger, no letter — an omission scholars call 'puzzling' and this artifact calls the whole point. Observe the legionary's posture: a man planting a joke he will not live to see land. It landed. It is still landing, in peer-reviewed journals, to this day.
designs.conservatorNote: The title is printed 'DODECAHEDRRONS,' double R, on the original. The registrar has elected to believe this, too, was deliberate. Under review.

designs.title: Edward Teach
designs.era: Golden Age of Piracy, final fiscal quarter
designs.region: Ocracoke Inlet, North Carolina, no forwarding address
designs.medium: Engraving on cream stock, fuses lit at time of printing
designs.edition: One per vow, vows not refundable
designs.placard: The gentleman wore lit fuses in his hair as a networking strategy, and it worked until it emphatically did not. The crossed pistols below are presented in the traditional 'his and also his' arrangement. The commitment advertised here was, records confirm, honored in full.
designs.audioGuide: The skull before you once belonged to a man who understood branding centuries before the term existed. Smoke curling from one's own head, it turns out, is a remarkably effective icebreaker. His final engagement at Ocracoke was attended by the Royal Navy, who did not RSVP. 'Till death' is the only promise in this wing that every party involved actually kept.

designs.title: Ferdinand
designs.tagline: If at First You Don't Succeed
designs.medium: Dense engraving on speckled cream, storm applied liberally
designs.edition: One expedition; eighteen finishers of roughly two hundred and seventy
designs.placard: Ferdinand Magellan organized the first voyage around the world and attended slightly more than half of it. The expedition succeeded; the distinction between the expedition and the man is left as an exercise. The skeleton rising from the waves is not named in the manifest.
designs.audioGuide: Consider the spyglass in the navigator's hand, aimed confidently at a horizon he would not personally reach. The proverb on this garment ends where Magellan did — mid-journey, in the Philippines, in 1521. His crew, demonstrating admirable initiative, finished the sentence and the circumnavigation without him. History awarded him the credit anyway, which is its own kind of cautionary tale.

designs.title: Franz Ferdinand — If at First You Don't Succeed
designs.placard: The Archduke's motorcade took a wrong turn. The first attempt that morning had already failed. History records what persistence accomplished; this garment records the proverb that should never have applied. The Archive extends its condolences to the concept of scheduling.
designs.audioGuide: Note the open car — a design choice historians describe as 'optimistic.' The driver's wrong turn placed the vehicle directly in front of a delicatessen, and the rest is the twentieth century.

designs.title: Get Wrecked With a Siren
designs.placard: Three sirens, one ship, zero survivors with complaints. The lyrics have never been transcribed because every reviewer joined the band. Odysseus heard the full set while tied to the mast and described it as worth it, which his crew, rowing with waxed ears, was not in a position to dispute.
designs.audioGuide: The sirens were not punished for singing; sailors were punished for listening, which tells you where antiquity assigned responsibility. Note the ship going down in the composition's lower register — less a tragedy than a five-star review. This print hung in the original CAUTIONARY TALES series and remains its most requested stop.

designs.title: Hannibal — Circle Back
designs.tagline: Let's take this offline. Over the Alps.
designs.placard: The Carthaginian general famously declined the direct route to Rome, electing instead to circle back through the Alps with war elephants. Rome, for its part, spent the next fifteen years asking him to put time on the calendar. He never took the meeting at Rome itself — history's most consequential unanswered follow-up.
designs.audioGuide: The elephant depicted survived the crossing, which is more than can be said for most of them. Patrons in middle management are asked to reflect on what 'circling back' cost the ancient world before using the phrase again.

designs.title: If At First You Don't Succeed
designs.placard: The expedition set out with five ships and roughly 270 men, and returned with one ship, eighteen men, and a very good story. Its commander is credited with the first circumnavigation despite completing, personally, slightly more than half of it. The globe pictured is the one thing that made the whole trip.
designs.audioGuide: Note the confidence of the pose — the confidence of a man consulting a globe that is wrong in several important places. The age of exploration ran almost entirely on this expression. Visitors are reminded that history awards the achievement to the voyage, not the survivor, which is either inspiring or a warning depending on your role in the fleet.

designs.title: JOHNNY DODECAHEDRRONS
designs.placard: A Roman folk figure wanders the frontier sowing regular dodecahedrons wherever the soil looks receptive. Scholars remain divided on what the objects were for; the figure himself appears untroubled by the question. The banner text — "Mysterious? Yes. Practical? No. Funny: Absolutelyt." — is preserved exactly as printed.
designs.audioGuide: You are looking at the Archive's only known portrait of the dodecahedron sower. Note the sack of twelve-sided bronze objects and the trail of them already planted behind him, none of which have germinated. Archaeologists have recovered over a hundred Roman dodecahedrons and produced zero convincing explanations. He said it would be hilarious, and two thousand years of baffled scholarship suggest he was right.
designs.conservatorNote: The doubled R in DODECAHEDRRONS and the terminal T of ABSOLUTELYT are original to the plate. The printer was likely also the humorist, which explains a great deal.

designs.title: MARIE ANTOINETTE
designs.placard: A kitchen appliance advertisement from a manufacturer with a poor grasp of the queen's final circumstances. The blade pictured is not dishwasher safe. The batter recipe, historians note, does not actually call for cake.
designs.audioGuide: Observe the elegant convergence of pâtisserie and public execution. The mixing bowl waits below the blade with a patience the crowd of 1793 did not share. Whether she ever said the famous line is disputed; whether the device worked is not. The museum's café serves brioche, out of respect.

designs.title: NEVER TRUST THE FAE
designs.region: Crossroads Hall, Route 66, Tarpon Springs, FL
designs.placard: An unredeemed ticket to a midnight set at Crossroads Hall. The fairy pictured was the opening act, the headliner, and the reason three attendees are still legally listed as 'elsewhere.' The jar was for donations. Do not ask what was donated.
designs.audioGuide: Twenty-five cents bought admission, though the fine print — visible only under moonlight — mentions additional fees. The thorn ring around the performer is a standard containment measure that has never once worked. Patrons who accepted drinks from the artist are asked to contact the registrar, in whatever decade they return.

designs.title: Phalynx
designs.placard: The phalanx worked on one principle: your shield protects the man beside you, and his protects you, brah. Three hundred men holding that thought at once conquered very little and are remembered for everything. The spelling has been adjusted for volume.
designs.audioGuide: The hoplite shield was carried on the left arm, covering half of its owner and half of his neighbor — the ancient world's most binding friendship contract. Break formation and everyone you love dies; hold it and you merely might. Unit No. 300 refers to a certain famous rearguard whose enrollment figure became a franchise. The Archive notes that 'got your back' was, for once in history, a literal job description.

designs.title: Rasputin
designs.tagline: That Which Does Not Kill You
designs.era: St. Petersburg, shortly before everyone ran out of patience
designs.region: Imperial Russia, dining room and river adjacent
designs.medium: Photo-engraving on mustard stock, poison-resistant inks
designs.edition: Repeated attempts; final edition eventually achieved
designs.placard: The sentence on this garment is famously unfinished, much like several attempts on the subject's life. Cyanide, gunfire, and a cold river each submitted their resignation in turn. The registrar has filed this under optimism, provisionally.
designs.audioGuide: Here we encounter the mystic in his natural habitat: the center of attention. His hosts prepared a tasting menu of poisons, and he reviewed it favorably. The proverb stops where it does because the second half kept being disproven. Visitors are encouraged to finish the sentence themselves, quietly, and at their own risk.

designs.title: Rasputin
designs.region: Yusupov Palace, then the Neva River, in that order
designs.medium: Bold woodcut on mustard stock, recruiting-poster posture
designs.edition: Statement accurate at time of printing
designs.placard: The mystic points directly at the viewer in the manner of a recruitment campaign, endorsing a claim with a strict expiration date. He survived the cake, the wine, and by some accounts the bullets. The river filed the final paperwork.
designs.audioGuide: The pointing finger is a device borrowed from wartime posters, and here it recruits the viewer into a shared delusion. For one remarkable evening, the statement printed below was repeatedly and stubbornly true. Poison failed. Gunfire underperformed. The registrar notes only that the claim, like the man, did not survive review.

designs.title: Rasputin
designs.tagline: I'm a survivor. Hey! Hey! Hey!
designs.placard: Poisoned, shot, and delivered to a frozen river, Grigori Rasputin maintained an attendance record most employees would envy. The portrait celebrates persistence as a personality flaw. His assassins filed no fewer than three completion reports.
designs.audioGuide: The gentleman before you was declared finished on several separate occasions, each announcement premature. Cyanide reportedly inconvenienced him no more than a heavy lunch. The Archive presents his portrait in the photographic style, as no woodcut could hold him either. He is, we are assured, gone now. We check periodically.
designs.conservatorNote: Executed in a photographic register that departs from the house woodcut idiom. Held for study while the curators debate whether the Archive's walls can accommodate a second visual dialect.

designs.title: Rasputin — I'm a Survivor
designs.placard: Poisoned, shot, and delivered to a frozen river, reportedly in that order, reportedly in one evening. The mystic's resume lists 'difficult to schedule' under special skills. The declaration reproduced here was accurate for longer than his assassins found convenient.
designs.audioGuide: Museum patrons should note that the historical record disputes nearly every detail of that night except the ending. The Archive presents the legend as received, because the legend is better.

designs.title: Robert Johnson — Saved
designs.placard: The bluesman who allegedly traded his soul at a crossroads for mastery of the guitar, depicted here in the one outcome the legend never considered. The Archive acknowledges this piece as something of a founding document. We are, after all, named for the venue.
designs.audioGuide: Below the figure, note the former counterparties to the transaction, visibly displeased. Contract law at the crossroads is notoriously one-sided, which makes this apparent breach the most remarkable legal outcome in Delta blues history.

designs.title: Roman Scorpio
designs.medium: Engraving on parchment, laurel flourishes, bolt included
designs.edition: Fired in volleys; minutes recorded by the survivors
designs.placard: The scorpio was Rome's precision bolt-thrower, capable of tabling a discussion at three hundred meters. A live scorpion perches on the bolt in what the engraver clearly considered a clarifying detail. The burning temple in the background indicates the agenda item was resolved.
designs.audioGuide: In the modern meeting, to put a pin in something is to defer it politely. The Roman legion took a more literal view of the idiom, and their pins were four feet long. Observe the machine's patient geometry — torsion springs, wheeled cart, everything a committee needs to reach consensus. The scorpion atop the bolt is believed to be a brand ambassador.

designs.title: ROMAN SCORPIO
designs.region: The night sky over the Empire
designs.placard: A legionary resolves an astrological disagreement with standard-issue equipment. The constellation above remained neutral. Roman horoscopes of the period were notably brief and ended the same way.
designs.audioGuide: Here the Empire's approach to the zodiac: identify the sign, pin the sign, file a report. The red plume indicates rank; the scorpion's expression indicates regret. The ornate botanical border was added later by a monk who felt the scene needed softening. It did not help.

designs.title: Rudis
designs.tagline: I'll Show You Mine, If You Show Me Yours
designs.medium: Distressed print on black, wooden sword rendered in cream
designs.placard: The rudis was a wooden sword presented to a gladiator upon earning his freedom, making it the only sword whose entire purpose was to never be used again. The proposition printed above it is, in this context, an invitation to compare retirements. Few could answer it.
designs.audioGuide: What you are looking at is a piece of wood worth more than any blade in the empire. A gladiator who received the rudis had survived enough afternoons of professional violence to be politely asked to stop. To show one's rudis was to show one's receipts. The arena arches behind it stand dark, having lost a valued employee.

designs.title: She's a Kraken
designs.placard: The lighthouse was built to warn ships away. The kraken interpreted it as playing hard to get. Marine authorities classify the relationship as 'entangled,' the lighthouse keeper's log classifies it as 'day 400,' and the kraken has not been asked, because nobody wants to make it weird. Weirder.
designs.audioGuide: Note the tenderness of the tentacle work — this is not an attack, which is somehow worse. Sailors' accounts of the kraken consistently describe something enormous that simply will not let go, and this print takes them at their word. The lighthouse continues to signal. The signal continues to be misread. Some relationships are like this.

designs.title: Spanish Empire
designs.medium: Gold-ground lithograph, two ochres and black
designs.placard: The chest holds more silver than Europe had ever seen, and Spain still managed to go bankrupt four times in one century. Importing a mountain of money turns out to devalue money — a lesson the Empire paid a mountain of money to learn. The coins spilling from the frame are trying to leave, as coins there always did.
designs.audioGuide: Between Potosí and the Manila galleons, Spain controlled the largest flow of precious metal in human history and converted it, with remarkable efficiency, into inflation and other people's wars. Economists cite the period as the first documented resource curse. The composition renders everything in gold tones because that was, briefly, the only color Spain could see.

designs.title: SPHINX — CAN I ASK YOU SOMETHING?
designs.placard: The Great Sphinx, smirking, opens with the seven most dangerous words in mythology. Historically, answering incorrectly was fatal. Historically, so was walking away. The Archive advises visitors that this is what riddles looked like before they moved to job interviews.
designs.audioGuide: The sphinx of legend posed her riddle to travelers outside Thebes and devoured those who failed, a customer-engagement strategy that has largely fallen out of favor. This rendering catches her mid-approach — note the smirk, weathered but intact after four and a half thousand years. "Can I ask you something?" has never once, in recorded history, preceded a comfortable conversation. The sphinx knows this. That is the whole point.

designs.title: THE GAUL
designs.edition: Crossroad Threads Tribal Tour, all dates cancelled by Caesar
designs.placard: A surviving tour poster from the 52 BC circuit, promoting an act the Romans reviewed poorly and then annexed. The animal skull was part of the stage set. The pine forest was not; it simply refused to leave.
designs.audioGuide: The Gallic warrior, seen here in full promotional regalia, headlined the woods for three centuries before Rome bought out the venue. Notice the skull at the base of the composition — merchandise, according to the tour manager. The final show at Alesia sold out. Nobody enjoyed it.

designs.title: The Ismailis
designs.placard: A second telling of the Archive's most polite death threat: the dagger and the note on the pillow, delivered while Saladin slept inside a guarded camp. The message was not the blade. The message was that they could have.
designs.audioGuide: You are looking at the companion plate to the Nizari holding one gallery over — same night, same tent, tighter crop. The pillow's embroidery reads 'you must,' the opening words of a sentence Saladin was left to finish himself. He lifted the siege of Masyaf shortly thereafter, citing scheduling. The curators keep the two plates apart, on the theory that the joke should not be able to see itself.
designs.conservatorNote: Square-format variant of the Nizari plate with the title and closing line cropped at the sheet edge. Held for study while the curators determine whether the crop is a wound or a choice.

designs.title: The Picts
designs.region: North of the wall, on purpose
designs.placard: Rome built two walls to keep these four men out, which the four men interpreted as a compliment. The lead figure is hoisting a rock — the Picts' primary export, argument, and musical instrument. No Pictish written records survive, possibly because writing things down was considered a sign of weakness.
designs.audioGuide: The tattoos are not decorative; they are documentation. Everything Rome knew about the Picts it learned at a distance and reported nervously. Observe the composition's energy — four warriors and a rock, arranged like a band photo, which is precisely the joke and precisely the history. Hadrian's Wall was, in this reading, the world's most expensive noise complaint.
designs.conservatorNote: Square-format impression; the title block sits close to the upper trim and shows minor cropping. Original as issued.

designs.title: WARWOLF
designs.edition: Crossroad Threads Siege Tour, one venue only
designs.placard: Promotional material for the largest trebuchet ever built. The garrison at Stirling attempted to surrender before the machine was finished; Edward I declined, having already paid for it. The projectile pictured is to scale. The castle, afterward, was not.
designs.audioGuide: Warwolf took three months to assemble and considerably less time to make its point. Note the graceful arc of the payload, described by one chronicler as 'educational.' The siege ended as sieges tend to when one side has a four-story catapult and the other has opinions. The tour was not extended.

designs.title: Wendigu
designs.region: The northern pine forests, far from the last settlement
designs.medium: Charcoal-textured print on tan, fog applied generously
designs.edition: Grows with every serving; never satisfied, never sold out
designs.placard: The gaunt figure among the pines is the folklore tradition's most direct nutritional advisory. The creature is said to grow in proportion to its appetite, ensuring it can never be full — a business model several industries have since adopted. The dietary guidance printed here is offered twice, for emphasis.
designs.audioGuide: Here, in the frozen timber, we find hunger given legs. The legend served as a hard rule for communities facing starvation winters: some meals cost more than they feed. Note the antlers, the visible ribs, the eyes like unattended lanterns. The maxim on this garment appears in kitchens across the continent, where it is taken considerably less seriously.
designs.conservatorNote: Name printed 'WENDIGU' — a deviation from the standard 'Wendigo' orthography, original to the print run. Tagline appears twice, top and bottom; believed intentional but noted for the record.

designs.title: British Empire
designs.region: Roughly a quarter of the map, in red
designs.medium: Three-color propaganda lithograph, crayon rendered at scale
designs.edition: Borders drawn freehand, consequences drawn out
designs.placard: A world map in the confident palette of a shipping company annual report, across which a crayon has been at work. The looping red line passes through several continents without consulting them. The question in the title was never put to a vote in the affected regions.
designs.audioGuide: The crayon is the largest object on this map, which the artist intends as a measurement rather than a joke. Many of the modern world's straightest borders were produced in rooms very far from the land they divided, often in an afternoon. The distinction between a doodle and a boundary, this print suggests, is mostly a matter of who is holding the crayon. The flag in the corner denotes the artist.

designs.title: Charon Charters
designs.placard: The underworld's oldest transit operator advertises three ports of call and a fare that has never once adjusted for inflation. Two cents, exact change, placed under the tongue. The bayou landing is a recent expansion; the ferryman found the climate agreeable.
designs.audioGuide: Charon has run the same route since before routes, and his pricing model — two obols, no refunds, no standby — has outlasted every currency that minted them. The lantern on the davit is not for the ferryman's benefit; he knows the way. It is for the passengers, who invariably want one last look. Note the third destination on the sign. The crossroads was always on the line; it just wasn't always printed.

designs.title: DIONYSUS WINERY & DISTILLERY
designs.placard: A promotional broadside for a vintner whose founder was famously torn apart and reassembled, a process the tasting notes describe as "complex." The distillery arm of the business is unlicensed in all mortal jurisdictions. Patrons are reminded that ecstatic frenzy is not covered by the tasting fee.
designs.audioGuide: Here we observe the god of the vine in his natural habitat: mid-toast, entirely unbothered. Note the barrel, aged to perfection, and the raised glass, which has never once been set down. Scholars debate whether the slogan refers to rebirth of the spirit or of the liver. The docents recommend not asking him directly.

designs.title: Dutch Empire
designs.era: Seventeenth century, listing photos taken earlier
designs.region: Wherever the galleon dropped anchor and raised the rent
designs.medium: Travel-poster lithograph, navy and cream, gold underline included at no extra charge
designs.edition: Open enrollment, existing residents need not apply
designs.placard: A galleon arrives under full sail, one of which is printed with an apartment floor plan. A bicycle is lashed to the deck, an omen locals learned to read too late. The gold underline lends the whole enterprise an air of legitimacy it did not otherwise carry.
designs.audioGuide: Watch closely as the vessel approaches the harbor. Within a generation, the trading post becomes a district, the district acquires a coffee shop, and the coffee shop acquires a waiting list. The floor-plan sail is the earliest known example of pre-construction marketing. The bicycle, naturalists note, always arrives first.

designs.title: HEPHAESTUS
designs.region: The forge under Mount Etna, rear entrance
designs.placard: A trade card for the pantheon's only licensed craftsman. The net shown is the model he once used to catch his wife and Ares mid-appointment, now offered in fishing weight. Repairs are honest, prices are fair, and the proprietor does not want to talk about it.
designs.audioGuide: The god of the forge holds his two bestsellers: a hammer that built the armor of Achilles, and a net with one very famous prior use. He pivoted to marine hardware afterward. The other gods laughed at the exhibition; the fishermen of the Aegean did not, and their nets never broke again. Business is good. The marriage is not discussed.

designs.title: Icarus Candles & More
designs.placard: A family business: the father handled engineering, the son handled marketing, and the marketing, as usual, won. The signature winged-dish candle is the flagship product and the incident report. Customer reviews average five stars right up until the wax point, after which reviews stop.
designs.audioGuide: The 'and more' in the company name is doing considerable legal work. Daedalus specified an operating altitude; Icarus treated it as a suggestion, which is the entire history of product misuse compressed into one flight. The candle motif inverts the myth cleanly — here, the wax holds the flame instead of failing it. Briefly. Everything in this shop works briefly.

designs.title: London Tower Motel
designs.placard: England's longest-operating hospitality venue advertises with characteristic understatement. Checkout times were historically flexible; check-ins were not. Several guests are still on the register.
designs.audioGuide: The Tower of London has hosted royalty, traitors, ravens, and the occasional overlap of all three. 'We've got room' is technically accurate — occupancy turned over briskly, particularly in the sixteenth century. Amenities included river views, an on-site chapel, and a green where certain reservations were concluded. The ravens are permanent residents; the management has strong feelings about their checkout.

designs.title: Never Trust the Fae
designs.placard: A public-safety notice in the tradition of communities that learned the hard way. The mason jar of iron nails is not a craft project; it is a perimeter. Do not step in circles, do not give your name, and do not accept the second cup of anything.
designs.audioGuide: What you are viewing is folk-era signage from a region that never filed incident reports, only warnings. The fairy silhouette is rendered small because the artist refused to render it accurately. Salt lines the doorways; cold iron lines the pockets. The Archive reminds visitors that the sign says 'never,' and the sign has not once been amended.

designs.title: PANDORA — BOX YOUR CHAOS
designs.era: The very beginning of everything going wrong
designs.placard: A period advertisement for the Pandora organizational system, shown here in its signature open position with contents actively escaping. The manufacturer's slogan, "Box up your troubles today!", tested well with focus groups who had not yet read the instructions. Hope sold separately, and last.
designs.audioGuide: The original Pandora unit shipped with a single, prominently labeled operating instruction, which was ignored immediately. What escaped is depicted here as tendrils; contemporary sources describe it as everything. Note the advertisement's cheerful mustard palette, standard for products that should never have been sold to the public. The one item that stayed in the box, hope, remains the company's best performing feature, largely because no one can get it out.

designs.title: ROMAN SCORPIO — PUT A PIN IN IT
designs.placard: The scorpio was Rome's precision bolt-thrower, capable of pinning a man to a gate at three hundred yards. Here it fronts a trade advertisement promising to "put a pin in it," which the legions meant considerably more literally than modern management does. Follow-up was guaranteed.
designs.audioGuide: Roman military engineers built the scorpio small enough for a two-man crew and accurate enough that defenders learned not to look over the walls. The phrase "put a pin in it" now means to defer something indefinitely; the scorpio deferred nothing. The Archive notes that a second impression of this advertisement exists in the collection, suggesting the campaign performed well. Repeat business, in this industry, meant something specific.

designs.title: Rudis — I'll Show You Mine, If You Show Me Yours
designs.placard: The rudis was the wooden sword presented to a gladiator upon earning freedom — the only piece of merchandise in the arena worth having. This tour shirt commemorates a season in which every performer was extremely motivated to headline the final show.
designs.audioGuide: The crossed gladii above the amphitheater are shown at actual bravado. Patrons will note the tour year: 79 AD was, for reasons involving Vesuvius, a difficult season for outdoor venues.
designs.conservatorNote: The registrar notes the artist's spelling of 'CROSSRROAD' on this piece. Whether printer's error or period-authentic detail, the Archive has elected to preserve it as found.

designs.title: VOIVODE OF WALLACHIA INTERIOR DESIGN
designs.placard: An advertisement for a design consultancy known for one signature installation, repeated approximately twenty thousand times along the roads of Wallachia. The magazine he is reading contains his own work. Client satisfaction was total, as no complaints were ever filed.
designs.audioGuide: The voivode's aesthetic philosophy favored strong vertical elements and outdoor installations visible from great distances — the Ottoman army viewed one such gallery in 1462 and elected to go home. Here he relaxes with the trade literature, chandelier lit, moon full, smile professional. 'Timeless Elegance,' the copy promises. The ellipsis is doing considerable work.

designs.title: Artemis
designs.placard: The challenge has one previous contestant. He is now a constellation, which the management considers a generous severance package. The deer is a spotter. The bow is not decorative. Applications are reviewed nightly, under the crescent moon, and the acceptance rate remains at exactly one hundred percent — everyone gets in, no one gets out.
designs.audioGuide: Artemis placed Orion in the stars either as tribute or as a warning, and the myth declines to clarify, which is very on-brand. Note the arrows rendered as constellation lines across the fallen hunter — the kill mounted in the largest trophy room available. The 'ask about our' framing is standard retail courtesy applied to a goddess with a documented no-refund policy.

designs.title: Artemis
designs.region: A mountain lake under a crescent moon, staff of one
designs.medium: Engraving in black and gold within a floral frame, stag included
designs.edition: Enrollment open, completion rate one hundred percent
designs.placard: This companion piece presents the goddess in profile, bow drawn, while the previous challenger flees across the sky in constellation form. The banner adopts the register of a small-business promotion. The stag at her side has seen the challenge administered and declines to participate.
designs.audioGuide: Every wilderness outfitter has a signature offering, and this one is no exception. The Orion Challenge has been attempted exactly once, and its results are displayed nightly at planetary scale. Notice the ornamental frame — flowers and filigree surrounding a woman in the act of permanent customer conversion. The stag's expression is best described as 'informed.'

designs.title: ARTEMIS
designs.region: The wilds of Arcadia; results visible in the night sky
designs.medium: Sepia engraving with red accent, on cotton
designs.placard: An engraving of the goddess of the hunt administering the Orion Challenge to its namesake and only contestant. He was later placed among the stars, which the fine print describes as a consolation prize. The challenge remains open.
designs.audioGuide: The rules of the Orion Challenge have never been published, as the first participant did not survive orientation. Note the drawn bow, held at full extension without strain — the goddess is not exerting herself, which contestants found discouraging. He got a constellation out of it. She got a slogan.

designs.title: Artemis: The Orion Challenge
designs.region: The night sky over the Aegean, northeast quadrant
designs.medium: Engraving in black and gold, drawn at full draw
designs.edition: One challenger accepted; see sky for standings
designs.placard: The goddess of the hunt is depicted mid-release, and a gentleman is depicted mid-descent. The motivational phrasing borrows from a poster genre the Greeks mercifully lacked. Orion's placement among the stars was, in the end, a promotion he did not request.
designs.audioGuide: Note the elegant economy of the huntress's form: laurel crown, drawn bow, complete indifference to the tumbling figure at upper right. The self-help industry advises us to shoot for the moon, for even if we miss, we land among the stars. Orion tested this claim empirically. The constellation remains visible on clear nights as a permanent participation trophy.

designs.title: Date a Silkie
designs.medium: Myth Series letterpress, cream and black
designs.placard: The silkie is a seal in the water and a heartbreaker on land, and the folklore is unambiguous about the schedule: they always go back. The lantern in this composition belongs to whoever is waiting, which is the only role the legend leaves open. Applicants should know the position is permanent and the silkie's is not.
designs.audioGuide: In the Orkney tales, the silkie's sealskin is the prenup — hide it and they stay, find it and they're gone by morning tide. Every version of the story ends at the shoreline, and this print has the good manners to start there. Note the seal and the woman rendered back to back: not two figures, but one figure and her exit strategy.

designs.title: Dionysus Winery & Distillery
designs.region: The vineyard under the Spanish moss
designs.placard: The god of wine appraises his own vintage beneath a live oak in Spanish moss, having relocated operations to friendlier growing country. The jug in the basket is the distillery side of the business and is not discussed with revenue agents. Tastings run from dusk until consequences.
designs.audioGuide: Dionysus was the only Olympian to hold dual citizenship between ecstasy and agriculture, which is to say, he ran a farm. Here he inspects the year's pour with the expression of a man who already knows it's good. The Spanish moss is not native to Thrace; the god goes where the growing season suits him. The Archive's insurers have asked us to state that the distillery is decorative. The Archive states no such thing.

designs.title: Dionysus, Diners & Dives
designs.tagline: Raise a glass to a wild life
designs.placard: The Live Oak Diner & Dive Bar promises three things — EAT, DRINK, SIN — and the god in the goblet has personally verified all of them. The muscle car outside is registered under the plate REVEL. The establishment has never once failed a vibe inspection, largely because no inspector has ever left.
designs.audioGuide: Observe the neon signage, still warm. The Greeks built temples to Dionysus; the American South, more practically, built him a diner with a full bar and a gravel lot. Note the serpent by the doorway — less a threat than a regular. Patrons are advised that 'last call' is understood here as a philosophical position rather than a scheduled event.

designs.title: DON'T PUT THAT HERMES
designs.placard: Hermes, patron of messengers, merchants, and travelers, holds the caduceus at maximum arm's length with an expression the Archive's registrar has catalogued as "profound reluctance." What he has been asked to deliver is not recorded. His face suggests we should not ask.
designs.audioGuide: The caduceus was Hermes' badge of office, carried on every errand between gods and mortals. In this rendering, the errand has clearly gone wrong. Note the recoiling posture, perfected over several millennia of delivering news nobody wanted. Modern couriers who have carried a durian through an office lobby report feeling, in their words, seen.

designs.title: Harpies
designs.placard: Two harpies, one bar, and a pint between them. Classical sources describe harpies as snatchers of food and tormentors of the blind; this pair has settled into semi-retirement as the most honest wingmen in mythology. They will absolutely tell you what they think of your prospects. That is the torment.
designs.audioGuide: The harpies' résumé includes ruining every meal King Phineus ever sat down to — a skill set that transfers directly to offering unsolicited dating commentary from a barstool. Note the pointing gesture: in the original myths this preceded theft, and in this composition it precedes advice, which witnesses describe as worse. The wings are literal. The wingman title, for once, is earned.
designs.conservatorNote: Square-format impression trimmed close on the upper and lower text blocks; 'HARPIES' and 'THE OG WINGMAN' each show edge cropping. Original as issued.

designs.title: Hermes
designs.placard: The god of commerce, travelers, and thieves raises one hand in the universal gesture of a man who has seen your packing job. The caduceus is not a decoration; it is a clipboard. Delivery windows are between dawn and never.
designs.audioGuide: Hermes moved messages, souls, and merchandise across three realms, which made him history's first logistics network and its first complaints department. The raised palm you see predates every loading dock it now governs. Note the winged sandals — standard issue, non-transferable. He has already seen where you were about to put that, and the answer remains no.

designs.title: HERMES
designs.tagline: Don't Put That There — The "Fell On It" Special
designs.placard: The messenger god, patron of travelers, thieves, and the medical caduceus he is pointedly gesturing with, offers a judgment-free consultation for a very common category of emergency-room visit. The special is named for the explanation every patient gives. He has heard it before. He does not believe it.
designs.audioGuide: Note the expression: not anger, not surprise — the weary professionalism of an immortal who has processed this exact intake form for three thousand years. The caduceus, two snakes on a staff, became the symbol of medicine by clerical error, which Hermes, god of tricksters, considers his finest work. The snakes have also heard your explanation. They do not believe it either.
designs.conservatorNote: The original print is cropped at head and foot — the title and final line of the slogan run off the sheet. The registrar has logged this as 'an injury consistent with the subject matter.'

designs.title: Hestia
designs.placard: Hestia never left home, never took sides, and never let the fire go out, making her the only Olympian with a perfect attendance record. The title is a base slander — she has never burned down anything. She simply maintains the one fire that matters and lets the rest of the family generate the drama.
designs.audioGuide: The most senior of the Olympians and the least depicted: Hestia gave up her throne to Dionysus to keep the peace, then went back to tending the hearth. Every Greek sacrifice began and ended with her name, which is the mythological equivalent of owning the utility company. The Doric column and brick arch here frame the fire the way the culture did — as the thing everything else is built around.

designs.title: It's All Unicorns & Hippogriffs
designs.medium: Heraldic silhouette print, black on kraft
designs.placard: The heraldic record is clear: the unicorn is decorative, the hippogriff is derivative, and the griffin is the original firm both were founded to compete with. Note the scale relationship — the griffin has allowed the smaller creatures into the frame, which heralds agree is the most menacing thing a griffin can do.
designs.audioGuide: Medieval bestiaries treated the griffin as fact and the hippogriff as satire — Ariosto invented the latter specifically as a joke about impossible things. This print restores the pecking order. Observe the wings at full spread: in heraldry this posture is called 'segreant,' and in every other context it is called 'the reason the unicorn is standing very still.'

designs.title: KALI — WORLD TOUR
designs.placard: The goddess of time, destruction, and renewal, rendered in the format of a concert tour shirt. Four arms, one skull garland, no listed venues. When your tour is the dissolution of the universe, every city is a stop and every date is sold out.
designs.audioGuide: Kali is among the most widely misunderstood figures in any pantheon: her destruction clears the ground for creation, which arguably makes this less a farewell tour than a permanent residency. Note the severed head, a standard element of her iconography representing the ego. Merchandise tables at the actual event have never been documented, which is why the Archive prints its own.

designs.title: Khani
designs.placard: Shani, lord of Saturn and karmic accounting, rides his crow through a ledger only he can read. Everything you have is on credit; he is merely the one who mails the statements. Payment plans run seven and a half years, no exceptions.
designs.audioGuide: In Vedic tradition, Shani does not punish — he invoices. His transit through your birth chart, the sadhe sati, lasts seven and a half years and functions as a comprehensive audit. The crow is his mount and, scholars suspect, his collections department. The Archive notes that the tagline is not a joke about luxury tailoring. It is a joke about everything.

designs.title: Narcissus
designs.medium: Single-ink stamp relief on cream stock
designs.placard: The subject discovered the first mirror with a lake attached and never logged off. Scholars debate whether he starved or simply refused to leave before getting the angle right. The caption was added posthumously; he would have approved.
designs.audioGuide: Here we find the myth compressed to a single ink and a single pose. Narcissus was not punished with vanity — he was punished with a reflection that loved him back exactly as much as he deserved. The economy of line in this print mirrors the economy of the myth itself: one boy, one pool, no survivors.

designs.title: NOT AS PICTURED
designs.placard: A consumer-protection notice concerning a deity whose profile pictures were consistently misleading. The bull, swan, and eagle shown were all, upon closer inspection, the same married Olympian. Refunds were not issued.
designs.audioGuide: Three animals, one god, zero accountability. The composition presents Zeus's most frequently deployed disguises in the flat, honest style of a catalog page — a style the god himself never once employed. Hera has asked that we not display the full list. It required a larger wall.

designs.title: Poseidon
designs.region: The Mediterranean, and one unexplained paddock
designs.medium: Woodcut on tan stock, trident planted with authority
designs.edition: Three domains, one of them on probation
designs.placard: The god of the sea holds a portfolio of oceans, earthquakes, and — following what scholars assume was a very late addition to the org chart — horses. The wave and the fault line share an obvious professional relationship. The horse stands slightly apart, aware of the question.
designs.audioGuide: Every large organization has one department nobody can explain. Poseidon's dominion over the sea is intuitive; his authority over earthquakes follows from the sea floor; and then, at the edge of the composition, there is a horse. Mythographers offer several explanations, none of which the horse has confirmed. It remains the pantheon's most successful scope creep.

designs.title: Prometheus
designs.medium: Full-color woodcut with skillet still life
designs.placard: He stole fire so mortals could cook, and the gods, with a sense of humor nobody credits them for, arranged for him to become the meal. The eagle arrives daily. The liver regrows nightly. The onions are a regional garnish. Eternity is mostly a menu problem.
designs.audioGuide: Of the three known impressions of this design, this is the definitive state — note the crescent moon, the chain work, and the cast-iron skillet in the lower register, plated with liver and onion rings in open defiance of good taste. Prometheus gave humanity fire and got a standing reservation he cannot cancel. The kitchen, as ever, is open.

designs.title: Prometheus
designs.region: The Caucasus, a rock with a view
designs.placard: Prometheus gave humanity fire and received, in exchange, a daily visit from an eagle with dietary preferences. The kitchen has taken the liberty of adding onions. The artist declined to look away, and so must you.
designs.audioGuide: Observe the eagle, punctual as grief, arriving at the same rock each morning. The chained figure regenerates overnight — a detail the gods considered mercy and Prometheus considered scheduling. Note the skillet in the lower register, an editorial flourish. The docents recommend viewing this piece before lunch, not after.
designs.conservatorNote: Third known variant of the Prometheus plate. Considerably more anatomical than its siblings; held for study while the curators determine which telling of the myth the Archive will stand behind.

designs.title: Prometheus (Study I)
designs.medium: Woodcut on speckled stock, second state
designs.placard: A second state of the Prometheus composition, pulled on speckled stock with the banner and skillet repositioned. The registrar retains it for comparison with the definitive impression, and because deaccessioning a Titan feels legally ambitious.
designs.audioGuide: Compare this state with the primary impression elsewhere in this wing: the eagle's posture is more upright, the moon renders smaller, and the onion still life takes a different arrangement. Printmakers pull multiple states to test what the image can survive — a methodology Prometheus himself would recognize.
designs.conservatorNote: Second of three known states of this composition. Retained for study; the definitive impression hangs in the Pantheon wing.

designs.title: Prometheus: Liver and Onions
designs.era: Eternal; service began shortly after the fire incident
designs.region: A diner on the slopes of the Caucasus, open 24 hours
designs.medium: Engraving on aged paper stock, checkered floor included
designs.edition: One plate, refilled daily by arrangement
designs.placard: The Titan who gave humanity fire slumps in a diner booth, still chained, while an eagle in a server's apron presents the daily special. The dish requires no menu, as it is always the same and always his. The coffee, at least, appears to be fresh.
designs.audioGuide: The punishment of Prometheus was engineered for maximum repetition: the liver regrows each night, and the eagle returns each day, a delivery schedule modern subscription services can only envy. Here the myth is reimagined as the bleakest lunch counter in Greek literature. Notice the eagle's apron — somewhere along eternity, the arrangement became simply a job. The onion rings are, by all accounts, an editorial addition.

designs.title: Roman Scorpio
designs.placard: A legionary shares the frame with a scorpion holding an actual pin, in the Archive's most literal act of project management. Scorpio was the emblem of the Praetorian Guard, who also specialized in tabling matters indefinitely. Some agenda items were emperors.
designs.audioGuide: The scorpion was the badge of the Praetorian Guard, chosen for its patience and its sting — qualities the Guard applied chiefly to their own employers. To 'put a pin in it' is modern office language for a decision deferred; the Praetorians preferred the dagger, but the principle held. Note the starfield: the eighth house governs endings, other people's money, and meetings that should have been letters. The pin, the curators stress, was always coming back out.

designs.title: RUDIS — I'LL SHOW YOU MINE, IF YOU SHOW ME YOURS
Shani
designs.medium: Screen print on tan cotton, photographed as worn
designs.placard: Shani, lord of Saturn, does not punish. He invoices. The crow is the delivery vehicle, the spear is the late fee, and the dotted rings mark the repayment schedule, which is measured in years and non-negotiable. Everything you did is on file.
designs.audioGuide: In Hindu astrology, Shani's transit through your sign — the sade sati — lasts seven and a half years, which is longer than most mortgages feel. He is feared not because he is cruel but because he is fair, and nobody's ledger is clean. The astrological rings in this composition are rendered as dotted lines: terms and conditions, orbiting.

designs.title: Shani
designs.era: Ongoing; statements issued every seven and a half years
designs.region: The orbit of Saturn, billing department
designs.medium: Woodcut on cream, black ink, interest compounding
designs.placard: The lord of Saturn rides a crow of considerable size and keeps meticulous accounts. Four arms allow simultaneous processing of deeds, misdeeds, appeals, and late fees. The snake overhead is not part of the collections process but does not discourage it.
designs.audioGuide: Here sits the most patient creditor in any pantheon. Where other deities deliver consequences on impulse, Shani prefers the installment plan: slow, thorough, and arriving precisely when least convenient. The crow serves as both mount and process server. Devotees traditionally offer sesame oil on Saturdays, which functions, roughly speaking, as a minimum payment.

designs.title: Shani — Karma on Credit
designs.tagline: The ledger is always balanced. Eventually.
designs.placard: Shani, the slow-moving one, keeper of consequences. He does not punish; he invoices. The ledger depicted here has never recorded an error, and the interest compounds in units the Archive's actuaries decline to calculate.
designs.audioGuide: The wheel beside the figure is not decorative. It turns once per lifetime, and the terms of service were agreed to at birth. Museum patrons who feel a chill in this gallery are asked to review their recent conduct.

designs.title: Shani — Karma on Credit (Garment Proof)
designs.tagline: The ledger is always balanced. Eventually.
designs.medium: Screen print on natural cotton, garment proof
designs.placard: A second state of the Shani design, pulled as a garment proof. The ledger is labeled, the tail of consequence is visible at lower right, and the astrological chart behind the figure has been rendered with the precision of an institution that never forgets a balance.
designs.audioGuide: Compare this proof with the flat impression displayed nearby. Conservators note minor differences in the cosmic chart between states. Shani noticed them first.

designs.title: Shapeshifter
designs.placard: Three panels document the king of the gods in his preferred disguises, arranged in order of escalating consequence. The middle figure is hooded because even the artist wanted deniability. Mortal viewers are advised that if an animal seems unusually charming, it is.
designs.audioGuide: The triptych format is borrowed from altarpieces, which is fitting, as each panel produced at least one demigod. Zeus's transformations were less about concealment than about plausible deniability, a legal strategy that predates law. The bull carried off Europa; the swan's business we describe only in footnotes. The third panel is labeled simply 'trouble,' which the curators consider the most accurate caption in the building.

designs.title: SPHINX
designs.region: Giza Plateau, outskirts of Thebes on weekends
designs.placard: A portrait of antiquity's most demanding gatekeeper. The questions were famously simple, provided you had never panicked in your life. Wrong answers were not returned.
designs.audioGuide: She sits, as she has always sat, on the rock of her choosing, waiting for a traveler with more confidence than preparation. The riddle about legs is the famous one; the follow-up questions are what got people. The museum does not require visitors to answer anything to exit this wing. Currently.

designs.title: Styx & Stones
designs.placard: The ferryman accepts exact change only. His route has one stop, his customer satisfaction scores are unrecorded, and his motto — reproduced here — is technically accurate, as life has never once broken him. The Archive notes he has excellent job security.
designs.audioGuide: The pole is original. The boat has been in continuous service since before service was invented. Patrons are reminded that the fare is traditionally placed under the tongue, and that the ferryman does not make change.

designs.title: Styx and Stones
designs.placard: An oar rests beside stepping stones in a river that requires neither. Words, the composition suggests, stopped hurting somewhere around the far bank. The ferryman was not consulted about the stones and remains bitter about the lost fares.
designs.audioGuide: Here the Archive presents its only known piece of underworld infrastructure criticism. The stepping stones represent a toll-free crossing — an innovation the ferry lobby suppressed for millennia. Listen closely and you may hear the water, which has heard every last word ever spoken and no longer flinches. The oar is decorative. It has always been decorative.

designs.title: Despite All Odds
designs.placard: A mother and daughter gather wildflowers beneath blossoming apple trees, a butterfly within the child's reach and a cottage that has clearly never known a mortgage. No plague, no pantheon, no fine print. The Archive acquired this piece under circumstances it describes only as 'despite all odds.'
designs.audioGuide: You have noticed this gallery feels different. The docents noticed too. There is no ledger here, no cursed lamp, no arrow mid-flight — only a good morning that was allowed to stay one. The committee keeps returning to it and leaving quieter than they arrived.
designs.conservatorNote: Curator's memo: rendered in soft pastels and oil light, entirely outside the house palette. Where Organism 46-B drifted dark, this one drifted kind. The committee has not decided whether tenderness is a wing or a visiting exhibition.

designs.title: LADY JANE GREY — 9 DAY JUICE CLEANSE
designs.placard: England's nine-day queen, reimagined as the face of a nine-day wellness program. She holds the mason jar with the composure of a woman who has read the fine print and signed anyway. The axe in the background is included at no additional charge.
designs.audioGuide: Here we find Lady Jane Grey, whose entire reign fit comfortably inside a modern juice cleanse. The Spanish moss draping the composition is not native to Tudor England; the Archive's founders imported it, as they import most things, for atmosphere. Observe how serenely she regards the viewer. She knows exactly how many days she has, which is more than most wellness customers can say.

designs.title: Marie Antoinette
designs.region: Versailles, then the Place de la Révolution
designs.placard: A recipe card in the housekeeping style: two eggs, one cup vegetable oil, a few royals. The guillotine is pictured for scale. The queen never actually said the thing about cake, but the recipe went ahead without her endorsement.
designs.audioGuide: The recipe-card format was chosen by the artist for its warmth, which is doing a great deal of work here. France in 1793 discovered that certain dishes require ingredients the pantry can only supply once. The portrait is period-accurate down to the pouf; the apparatus beside her is period-accurate down to the angle of the blade. 'Homemade made easy' — the Revolution's entire theory of government, on one card.

designs.title: MARY, QUEEN OF SCOTS
designs.region: Scotland, France, and an English scaffold
designs.placard: Mary Stuart presents a Bloody Mary garnished with celery, bacon, shrimp, and a full slider, a load-bearing brunch cocktail for a load-bearing life. Historians note the drink is named for a different Mary entirely. The Archive has elected not to correct her.
designs.audioGuide: Mary, Queen of Scots was crowned at six days old, widowed at eighteen, and executed at forty-four, a résumé that more than earns the garnish. Observe the Tudor ruff, immaculate, and the cocktail, structurally ambitious. Brunch scholarship holds that a garnish should never exceed the drink in caloric value. Mary, characteristically, did not consult the scholarship.

designs.title: OLGA OF KIEV
designs.tagline: She Mourned Her Husband in Pieces
designs.era: 945 AD, a bad year for the Drevlians
designs.placard: Portrait of a grieving regent holding a torch, for light. The trees behind her were not always burnt. The Drevlians sent two delegations of suitors; neither filed a return itinerary. She was later made a saint, which everyone agreed was the safest option.
designs.audioGuide: Widowhood is observed differently across cultures. Olga's observance involved a bathhouse, a funeral feast, several birds carrying embers, and the systematic end of a rival state. The Church canonized her for her later works, the torch not among them. Note the composure. It is the most frightening element in the frame.

designs.title: ROMANOV SPA DAY
designs.placard: Promotional material for a wellness experience held in a cellar with excellent stonework and poor exits. Amenities included a claw-foot tub, complimentary slippers, champagne on a stool, and history. The spa did not accept future bookings.
designs.audioGuide: Note the careful staging: candles on the ledge, steam rising, a towel draped just so. Every element promises rest. The guillotine in the corner is not period-accurate to the Romanovs, which the establishment considered a comforting detail. It was not. The slippers, however, were genuinely very soft.
